summary: Jeff Atwood documents his process of rebuilding his 2.5-year-old PC with a new Intel Core i7 platform, arguing that building and upgrading your own PC maximizes value over time. He walks through the component selection process, explaining why the i7's triple-channel memory, true quad-core architecture, and maturing software ecosystem finally made upgrading worthwhile. The post serves as both a practical guide to PC rebuilding and a philosophical defense of DIY computing, emphasizing that hardware upgrades are cheap compared to programmer time. He details the $1000 rebuild process step-by-step, from assembling components outside the case to the first boot and BIOS update.
key_points: Building your own PC pays dividends through incremental upgrades over time, similar to buying versus renting a car
The Core i7 platform's six memory slots enable 12GB RAM without expensive DIMMs, making it a practical upgrade from Core 2
Hardware costs are trivial compared to programmer time - saving 15 minutes daily easily justifies $200-500 upgrades
PC building is fundamentally simple - 'if you can assemble a LEGO kit, you can build a PC'
A platform rebuild (motherboard, CPU, RAM, heatsink) transforms an old system into state-of-the-art while keeping case, PSU, and storage
money_quotes: A big part of the value proposition of building your own PC is upgrading it in pieces and parts over time.
Normal computers age in dog years – every year they get seven years older.
As far as I'm concerned, until everything on my computer happens instantaneously, my computer is not nearly fast enough.
Don't ask me why I need six cores and 24GB. To paraphrase a Zen master, if you have to ask, you do not know.
Hardware is Cheap, and Programmers are Expensive.
If you can assemble a LEGO kit, you can build a PC.
On the outside, it will appear to be the same car, but there's a lot more horses under the hood.
Forget the wattage rating and look at the quality.
key_insight: Rebuilding your own PC is a cost-effective way to maintain top-tier performance, because hardware investments are trivial compared to the value of a programmer's time.
